speaker: interviewer
text: --with survivor Sally Muschel. And it's tape number 4.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'I remember. I will never forget. I remember my house, my home, each street, each stone. I remember the loving softness of my mother, the gentle kindness of my father. I remember those Friday nights, our big table with poor strangers sitting around. They sat with sadness, hardly making a sound. I hear my father's soft voice. Please, eat some more. I seen their eyes, tears glow. I remember sitting on my father's lap listening to stories from the [HEBREW], our Torah glories. I remember my father's voice, so gentle, so kind. Remember, Sally, be always charitable. Have the poor in mind. I remember the happiness, the joys, playing with friends and sharing toys. I remember the seasons in my sweet home, my little heart bursting with glee. I remember spring, the scent of the flowers, running, singing, dancing for hours. I remember the rose garden nearby. I can still see the bird in the summer sky. I remember autumn. A leaf fell on my head. My mother brush from my hair. I shouldn't be bothered by it. I remember the winter trips to the high mountains, riding in sleds in cold, crisp air. The snow was so white, so pure as diamonds. Oh, I feel so good, so warm on my mother's breast. I remember father's lessons. They were always the best. And glow of pride I saw in his eyes when I recite [HEBREW] by heart to his surprise. But black clouds gather around the world, clouds of hate, clouds of fear. Ghastly strong winds of war came our way, destroying my dreams, happiness, and all that's dear. They turn everything to dust and ashes and blow it away. My mother and father whisper, murmur. They wander and worry, one thing on their mind-- how to protect, how to save our child. Our neighbor is packing, the Russian border his goal. Then the idea. He's our friend. With him our only child we would send. No matter his failing. He's [? barely ?] Jew. For saving our only child, the choices are few.

speaker: interviewee
text: Will you pack few things? There is no time for more. Sally, with our friend you must go. I scream and I cry. I will be good. I will behave. I promise. I promise. Please, don't send me away. They hug me. They kiss me. They promise me the world. They whisper in my ear, don't cry, baby. Dry your-- dry your eyes, dear. I keep on crying. I want to stay home. And they said through their tears, sweet child, you must go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my mother kisses, my father's words. Remember, remember, my dearest child, the winds are so strong, and the world is so wild. Whatever will be is not ours to know. So remember, my child, your father's last words. No matter what life has in store for you, remember, my child, remember it well, that you are a daughter of Israel. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember. I remember forever and ever their set faces with eyes full of tears. The pain burns their heart. Their trembling hands wave good-bye, not knowing that I see them for the last time. I remember the pain. I feel it today. Oh, the pain, the pain rips a hole in my heart. I endure so much, too much to go through. The nights held such terror. The days held despair, gloomy and blue. A child alone, I was lonesome and frightened, forlorn among strangers, suffering life's cruelty and danger. With pain in my heart and lump in my throat, I dreamed of the day when I could go home. I dreamed a dream of a lonely child. I dream I'm home. I feel the warmth of my mother's chest. She holds me so close to her heart, tender with her love. Oh, it feels so good, so sweet, so right. I'm so happy today just now. But in the darkness of the night, I remember so keen, separating reality from the mirage, the dream. Oh, I remember, remember the pain. And then one day at the beginning of May, the shouts and cries. The war is over. There is a break in the clouds of a newborn day. I hope to see a beam of light in the dark sky, but my hopes are soon smash. My bubble bursts. I learned what I feared most. I'm an orphan, alone in the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: History set as fact. You have my word. I never will forget. Those whom I love have perished, are gone. They were consumed in a hellish fire among the 6 million innocent lives to satisfy a German's [INAUDIBLE] desire. And I cry and I cry and I cry. The children have a right to cry. I want to go on, to start a new life. The pain in my heart is cold as a storm. Will the pain in my heart ever be gone? The memories linger, and life goes on. I remember one day when I held our daughter dear, and all of a sudden I trembled with fear. Would I be strong enough to give away my child, even to save her life? No, no, not in a million years. I am too selfish. I like-- I lack the courage. I am putting it to rest. I can't send my child away from the nest, not even to save her life. I am selfish. I am selfish. I am selfish. I am selfish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I remember. Now I can feel the pain of a mother and father. I want to wipe the tears from their eyes and say, my parents dear, I'm alive. I'm alive. I'm alive. I love you so much for yesterday, today, and tomorrow. Now I can feel your pain and sorrow. And I cry and I cry and I cry. Many years pass by. The ashes are blown away. Many years pass by and then another day. We set our table, children, the children, yes. The candles are shining. The silver the best. I look at our grandchildren with pride and joy. Thank god, I say, now I have seen a beam of light in the dark, in the cloudy sky. Dear father, I appreciate your advice. I watch my children, grandchildren. They live by the Torah. They are so good, so kind. I listen to their discussion about [HEBREW]. I looked up to the heavens, my heart full of joy. And I whisper, dear father, I remembered. I remember too well that I am a daughter of Israel. And I cry and I cry tears of joy. I have been saved from the fires and ashes to continue life. I know you cry from heaven. That was our wish. That was our desire. And you are sending your kisses, your tender love, and a smile. And I smile and I smile and I smile.' Dedicated to my dear husband, children, and grandchildren in memory of my dear parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I want to thank you for sharing it with us. And we're going--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's very beautiful. So thank you. And we're going to look at photographs now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and myself. I was here approximately like five years old. I think I was five years old, because my mother looks pregnant here, so because she wasn't so heavy. So must it to be she--she was pregnant with my brother, and he was five years younger than I was. So I presume I was five years old here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was in Israel. My uncle had the photo, so that's how I got it. Because I didn't have any pictures at all. I don't have any picture of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture, from left to right, the first is a cousin of mine, which lived in Dabrowa, the same town but not with us. Her name was also [? Sala. ?] The next was my cousin Bayla, which lived with us. Next to Bayla it's me. And next to me it's another cousin, but-- which they went to England-- to Belgium, which I think it was either. I'm not sure. Standing is her brother, which I also am not sure which one it is because she had two brothers. But I think that's [NON-ENGLISH], his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The little one is me, The third from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother's oldest sister, because they were 11 children. My mother was the youngest. And this is her sister with her-- with her husband. They were in England. And I got the picture from them-- from the uncle in Israel. He gave me the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [? Toby ?] and Bernard [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother's next to the youngest brother. He was-- he was just before her, and with his wife. They lived in Germany, and they immigrated to Israel. And he was the one which had all the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Bernard and Helen Muschel. This is my cousin Bayla, which we grew up together. She was to me like a older sister. We lived together. We grew up together. And this is her uncle, Moishe Kenel, which he took care of me from January 1940 till March 1940. And this is her uncle. Her father passed away when she was a little girl. So that's why she lived with us. This is me, which it was-- the picture was taken in Russia, and I think this was in, like, 1944. I'm not sure [INAUDIBLE] was set up, but I think it was 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took the picture? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. Somebody was making pictures there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my brother-in-law was making those pictures. Yes he was making here the camera. He was making the pictures-- my husband's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the-- the bottom line from left to right, the left is me. This was, of course, what you were taking, like, during the war, they were teaching you first aid. You had to take this [INAUDIBLE]. But the other names I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, I think, was in [NON-ENGLISH] or in-- I-- I don't really remember. I don't remember where it was. Maybe in Iakutsk. I really don't-- the second time we came to Iakutsk, maybe the second time where we came to Iakutsk. I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already after the war, and they were sending us back to Poland. I am the one-- the door, one of the-- the other ones I don't remember who they were. We are loading the wagons to go to-- back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they the same kinds of wagons that were used for transports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- those are the wagons we [INAUDIBLE]. But these wagons weren't as bad as the wagons where we went to the-- to Siberia. Those were not already less people. And they were also cattle wagons, but they were clean, and there were less people in a wagon. And they let us go out, you know, on the way to-- to-- that we'd be able to buy something or to cook something. They were open all the time. But still, they were cattle wagon. Well, in Russia, everybody goes in the cattle wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the wagon where we were going back. They were sending us back to Poland from [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were loading the wagons. I am there, the second from the left in the dark dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our wedding picture, which actually was made the day after the wedding, because we had to go to the door of the photographer. He should make us the picture. So we went. I got dressed, and we made the picture. It was 1948, September 22. So the picture was made September 23. This is made in Lechfeld in a school which I was working, or it was in the kindergarten. And I am the one what's standing bent over the table with the children with the braids. This is also in Lechfeld. We went on the outing with the children. And I am the one in the front to the left with a wonderful group of children. And back there bending over is another girl, which I don't remember. I think [? Schwimmer ?] was her name. This is a English class in the DP camp, Ainring, and I am the first from the right. And the teacher's name was [? Mazurek. ?] And we learned English there. This was in Lechfeld, the old school. And I am the first from the right learning how to sew on the machine, on a sewing machine. This picture was taken on [NON-ENGLISH] bar mitzvah, and this is our whole family. Sitting from left to right is first my granddaughter [? Reena, ?] Next to her is [? Sheva ?], then is [? Frumie ?], then is [? Yonnie ?], [? Esty, ?] and Debbie. Standing from left to right is Chaim, Dr. Bernstein, Chaim Bernstein, which is my son-in-law. Next to him is Annie, our daughter. In middle is my husband. Then is [? Yossie ? ], the bar mitzvah boy. And then next to him am I. And then is my daughter Brenda and her husband Mark, Mark Plotnik. And this is our family-- seven grandchildren, four children, and grandmother and grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my husband Bernard Muschel, a husband for 48 years. And thank God, we are a very happy couple, and soon we're going to celebrate our, hopefully, our 50th anniversary. We have a wonderful family because I have a wonderful husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] very introducing me. [LAUGHS] I appreciate.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is on the-- this is already on the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I really appreciate. I just hope the way she was talking now, she wasn't too much excited, all this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, also the interview--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --I'm very happy that we made, because I really want it should be for grandchildren, for the future generation. They should know from what happened and how we survive and what kind life we had. And it's important. At the beginning, we couldn't tell them anything at all, because they were asking, why we won't have a grandparent? Why don't we have anybody? And was very difficult to tell them why. I mean, my both sister-in-laws had, even though they had from my husband's parents, but they still didn't have anybody from my family. And my both sister-in-laws, thank god, one of them had the whole family. The other one is a survivor, but they survived five sisters and a brother. So they had a big family with big cousins and everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my children were always, how come everybody has somebody and we don't have nobody? And it was difficult to tell them. Like now with the tape they would look at it. They will have something to show to their children, the grandchildren. It's going to go from generation to generation. Well, I want to thank you both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
